comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Franz Anton Mesmer, Austrian physician, occultist and Rosicrucian initiate, who publicized "animal magnetism" in late 18th-century France -- yes, that's pronounced "ki" in Japanese and "nwyfre" in old-fashioned literary Welsh. After his death, his system of energy work was stripped of its occult dimensions and of every reference to the life force to produce the first draft of what we now call "hypnotism." 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 712,874th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

replies.title: horse-head dream
replies.content:
Hi, anonymous. I have been interpreting my own dreams for many years and have participated in a dream group for a couple of years, so maybe I can be of some help. I more or less follow Jeremy Taylor’s methods; in this school of interpretation, no-one can dictate to you what your dream means — only you have the power to know what it means — but I can give you feedback in the form of “here’s what this dream would mean .” In other words, I’m giving you my reaction to your dream as if it were my dream, rather than explaining as if from on high what your dream means. I hope that makes sense.

If anything I say gives you an “aha!” kind of feeling, run with that. If what I say makes you go “meh, whatever,” then go with that.

Okay, so I had a look at your dream when you posted it the other day. First, the fact that the dream has repeated would indicate (if it were my dream) that it’s something important and the dreamworld is being quite insistent that I pay attention to this. If it were my dream, I would also think about the emotional tone of the dream. It sounded kind of detached in your description.

When I was a teen, I had a number of dreams with intensely disturbing imagery, yet a detached emotional tone. I think that was because the subject matter of the dream was too intense for me to take on both the visual imagery and an intense emotional tone at the same time. It was like the detached tone was protecting me from something. Or, it could be that it was pointing out that I had detached from my own emotions around what was being done to me in waking life. Or it could be both at the same time. If this were my dream, the fact that the dream is repeating and yet the tone is detached would probably mean that I have detached emotionally from something that is, under the surface, having a big impact on me.

Another point about dreams, for me, is that all dreams have multiple levels of meaning, and they don’t always appear to agree with each other. Interpretations of a dream can appear to conflict with each other, yet paradoxically they can all be true at once. Sometimes you just have to sit with those contradictions. There isn’t necessarily a single, all-encompassing meaning that is easy to put your finger on.

I’m going to take your past with horses as part of my interpretation, even though unlike you I was obsessed with horses as a kid ;).

So, for me, horses have to do with transportation and power. Knights rode on horseback, and that gave them an enormous advantage over foot-soldiers. Horses allow humans to go longer distances in a shorter amount of time, and they allow us to do work we couldn’t easily do by ourselves, like plow fields and transport goods. So here you have a symbol of animal power on top of a woman’s body.

For me, that would probably be saying something about my relationship with my body in waking life. Or my relationship with my own “animal power.” Perhaps I have put this power out to pasture.

In your family, according to what you wrote, your father (an authority figure presumably) viewed horses as mere tools. That puts another spin on the idea of the horse as a symbol of power. It’s power, but in your background, there’s that tool element, as if the being-ness of the horse has been denied. What does that mean in this context? I don’t know, but it feels like there’s something there about real power being discounted and ignored.

For me, there has always been an idea of riding and working with horses as a kind of mystical connection with an animal. Like, when you ride a horse, you’re more than just yourself. Riding a horse is not like riding a bicycle. A horse is a being, not a machine, and when you work with horses, you’re working with a conscious being that can choose not to cooperate with you. If this were my dream, my own feelings about horses would be in direct contradiction with my personal authority figure’s ideas about horses (horses as mystical animal connection vs horses as tools).

And then, there’s the fact that your dad made an exception for Rebel. The fact that you brought up Rebel in connection with this dream seems significant to me. Also, think about the name "Rebel" and what that means in connection with the dream...

The blue dress. First, dresses are traditional female garments in many cultures. So does this figure represent something about traditional femininity? Blue could relate to healing, sadness, the Virgin Mary, the sky, the sea…

I don’t know much about German traditions (as in Penn. Dutch) about horses and women, but I do know that there are several important Celtic traditions like the Irish story of Macha (a goddess of sovereignty) and Welsh Rhiannon. Horses figure prominently in a number of Celtic goddess traditions. So for me, we have echoes of power, sovereignty, goddess, traditional femininity, yet at the same time we have echoes of “put out to pasture,” a tool that is not being used, something that is waiting. Something that is blue.

Finally, for me this could have a double meaning of: the horse-headed woman is an aspect of myself, and at the same time, the dream has a visitation-like feeling, as if a horse goddess is trying to wake me up.

I hope this is helpful.

Tori Minard

If it were my dream, I would ask the dream character how its blue dress feels. I might even ask to feel what it is like to wear that blue dress. Find a blue dress and put it on, or at least examine it with great curiosity.

Or I would find an element of the dream that was particularly spooky and inquire into that in this way.

Well, folks, last night it was Bruce Springsteen (with his own head) performing “Ahab the Arab.” The night before, it was trying to get somewhere, I forget where, that required finding my way out of a mall and through a series of rich Americans’s enormous lawns. No one called the police or fired upon me, they were all very helpful (as I said, it was a dream). I forget the details of the night before that but they were equally weird. Since this has been a fertile field for fiction over the years, I am not going to mess with it. I’m going to start writing them down with a view to selling them.

And if anybody knocks, I will not answer the door.

Before this, I only ever remembered one dream, of being in a mansion with a terrifying air of menace, completely unarmed and, as far as I could tell, completely alone. This place was worse than Hill House and the Overlook Hotel put together. I remember being so frightened that I went and got the Bible, a rosary, and a crucifix and put them in the little bookcase on the headboard. If Stephen King had had that dream, he’d have gone to town with it and he’d now be a bajillion dollars richer than he already is. For 25 years I couldn’t even think about that house without feeling nervous, as stupid as that sounds. One night I woke up thinking about it and even that spooked me so much I checked to make sure the guns were loaded. Then I woke up a little more and thought, “You idiot, you can’t shoot ghosts.” So I put the holy ammo, as it were, on the headbord and went back to bed.

comments.title: Crossroads
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer and thank you again for your kind hosting of this forum and generous answers.

I've been wondering about the significance of crossroads recently after a confluence of "unrelated" mentions of the concept.

They seem to be sources, connections, spiritual gateways, good, bad, and usually definitively "other" in most mentions.

But, cities are full of crossroads (e.g. "intersections") and we don't hear so much mention of these as having the same sort of meaning.

Are "intersections" different from "crossroads", and how best to understand this city/rural difference? Are crossroads actually all they're made up to be, and does it depend on your religion, culture, geographic location, or particular flavour of magic?

replies.title: Re: Crossroads
replies.content:
As I understand it, the lore surrounding crossroads is a folk memory of archaic traditions involving energy paths ("ley lines"). Old roads in many parts of the world followed the lines of the telluric current, and where they crossed, a cairn, standing stone, or some other marker offered a way for the knowledgeable to tap into the current for various purposes. City streets aren't generally laid out according to the earth currents, so don't have the same effect!

There are a lot of things like this in folk magic. Superstition -- literally things "standing over" (super-stitio) or, as we'd say, hanging over from an earlier time -- is very often the shell of a magical working whose essence has been misplaced over time.

replies.title: Re: Crossroads
replies.content: Is there any way you can tell where ley lines are, besides crossroads?

replies.title: Re: Crossroads
replies.content:
Pick up copies of Alfred Watkins' books The Old Straight Track and The Ley Hunter's Manual, study them carefully, and then go looking.

One thing to keep in mind, though, is that leys don't exist everywhere in the world. They're not natural phenomena -- the natural flows of the telluric current follow curves, as you can find by studying any good classical text of feng-shui. The leys were created in certain parts of the world, a long time ago, using methods of working nobody knows any more, for reasons that are just as thoroughly forgotten these days. As a general rule, you find leys where you find standing stones and other megalithic sites -- in northwestern Europe, northeastern North America, Japan, and certain other places scattered around the world.

replies.title: Re: Crossroads
replies.content:
The 3 listed are the most technologically advanced/Westernized places. Do you think the ancient cultures may have had similar features still relevant to that?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: Crossroads
replies.content: Good question! I don't happen to know the answer.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not just people on the left. That's a classic example of a thoughtstopper -- a verbal noise people use to keep themselves from thinking -- and there are plenty of examples across the political, social, and cultural spectrum. It's standard for them to be stereotyped -- people pick them up from examples they see online or in the media, and use them by rote, since that way they don't have to engage their minds enough to find a thoughtstopper.

Sometimes the best response is a counter-thoughtstopper. The next time someone does that in an online conversation with you, try, "So you're ignoring the examples I gave you. Got it." Do that word for word every single time, and other people will pick it up and make hay with it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may, there are a few things to note here:

(1) Rubeus in the first house is one of the classic warning signs in geomancy. While I will not personally go so far as some of the Renaissance texts to interpret this as "burn the chart and don't try again for at least two hours," it does indicate that the querent is not being entirely honest with the geomancer, or with himself. You might reflect on whether there are hidden motives here, whether you are testing the oracle vis-a-vis a decision you've already made, etc.

Rubeus in both house I and the house of the quaesited (here, house X) is traditionally a sign that the querent is deliberately lying to the geomancer.

It's probably best to stop here. But if you would prefer to go on anyway...

(2) The Judge is Amissio, which says either "yes, you should quit," or "yes, you are going to quit."

(3) Provided that the concerns in point (1) do not apply, then you are correct about the I/X perfection. It's an affirmative answer: yes, quit. As to the quality of the figure itself, (a) see point (1) above, and (b) Rubeus can mean an intense, "burn it all down and start again" kind of energy, or a kind of tunnel vision which is passionately focused on the specific goal to the extent of causing unintended consequences elsewhere due to single-minded carelessness.

(4) The Sentence (Cauda Draconis) speaks to some salient part of the outcome. Quitting will mean putting a final ending on things, and perhaps burning bridges in a way that there's no going back. It will also quite likely be a transition to a challenging time ahead (think of the South Node in astrology here), and may speak to regret down the line.

There's also another line on Cauda Draconis, which aligns with the concerns raised in (1) over Rubeus in I and X. It's traditional that Cauda in the first house means that the querent has already made up his mind. While here it is, instead, in the Sentence, I am concerned that some of that meaning may be in play, especially given the situation with Rubeus. Thus, the answer given by the I-X perfection, and also shown the Judge and Sentence, all gives a final judgement: If things continue as they are, you're going to quit, and that was true even before—and regardless of the outcome of—this geomantic reading.

Good morning and thank you for this open forum!

Over the holidays last year I received a Tarot deck in a dirty santa gift exchange. It was a novelty Vampirella themed deck. I have been using the method outlined in the MM FAQ to learn the deck for about a week now and have a couple of questions.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Deck Questions
replies.content:
1) If you like the deck and it seems to work for you, it's good. I've seen people use the most preposterous decks and cast good readings with them.

2) Always, always, always use your own intuitive impressions. The LWB is disposable.

3) It's a personal thing. I use reversed readings, and in my practice they're normally the reverse of the upright meaning -- so if you draw the Lovers, let's say, which I read upright as a combination or joining of factors, reversed it means separation or division. But your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Deck Questions
replies.content:
1) If you like the deck and it seems to work for you, it's good. I've seen people use the most preposterous decks and cast good readings with them.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Deck Questions
replies.content: Fair enough!

replies.title: Re: Tarot Deck Questions
replies.content:
For what it's worth:

1) If it came with a little white book of keywords, that means it was actually designed for divination; having a light-hearted rather than serious attitude doesn't stop that from being true. But in general, it's fine to use things that weren't meant for divination - most people for centuries used ordinary playing cards, flipped coins etc.

2) My take-away from years of doing this: that impression is the 'real' reading. You've seen each card many times before, but this time something different jumps out at you, or you see it in a different light, in a way that expresses a meaning unique to the question. That's how the cards can speak to every new situation that comes up, as opposed to saying the same few things over and over! The one thing I find keywords good for is to help me notice details about the symbolism that I hadn't spotted on my own.

3) What reversals mean is sort of an extension of the previous answer. Does the card 'say' something different to you when you see it upside-down, or not? Do you notice different things about the picture by seeing it from that angle? Does the image have symbols that take their meaning from being above/below something else, so that they end up switched if you draw the card upside-down? Is the card turned so that something on it is facing/pointing towards another card, or away from it? Or does none of this seem to make any difference? (Playing cards are usually the same both ways up, for example.)

comments.title: Dolmen Arch
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have spent the last year practicing the SOP and (Tarot) divination as part of my occult training. Many thanks to you for making all of these known through the Druidry Handbook and your other writings. I have gotten a lot of value out of the discursive meditation techniques and I am really appreciative to you for that!

Looking ahead to the next year or two, I am planning my occult studies and was hoping you would clarify the differences in a couple of paths, and where their emphasis lies. One branch of study would be to follow up the basic Druidry Handbook practices with the material in the Druid Magic Handbook, and the Druid Grove Handbook.

I am unclear on where the Gnostic Celtic Church fits in, and also how that differs from the Dolmen Arch course that is coming out hopefully later this year. Are these all complementary materials in the same vein, or do they really go in different directions for the practitioner?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch
replies.content:
The GCC is a system of spiritual practice that's compatible with The Druidry Handbook and its sequels, including the Dolmen Arch. It centers on a communion ritual that's performed regularly by the practitioner to deepen his or her spiritual connection with the cosmos and the deities he or she worships. A priest or bishop of the GCC is basically the equivalent of a monk or nun, following a personal Rule rather than one established or imposed by some outside authority, and practicing a hermit's vocation while in the midst of the world. You don't have to be ordained or consecrated by the GCC in order to perform the communion ceremony or explore the possibility of a vocation of that kind; ordination simply means accepting that vocation, and consecration gives you the power to pass on the quirky apostolic succession we've got.

The Dolmen Arch, by contrast, is a 200,000-word study course in old-fashioned occultism, full of philosophy, themes for meditation, various kinds of mental training, and (toward the end) a system of energy healing and a variety of other neat things. It's also wholly compatible with The Druidry Handbook and its sequels, and with the GCC.

The most important thing to remember is that these things are a buffet of options. If you're a Druid working in the broad AODA tradition, whether or not you're a member of AODA, you can pick up The Druid Magic Handbook, or The Gnostic Celtic Church, or The Dolmen Arch, or all of them, or none of them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, whoever wrote that was bad at math. According to the Population Reference Bureau, there have been more than 108 billion human beings born on this planet since our species evolved, and there are 7.6 billion alive today, so the total human population now is about 7% of the total number of people who have ever been alive.

Second, according to Druid teaching, there's a steady supply of souls entering the human stage all the time. We all start out our incarnations as single-celled organisms, learning the simplest lessons of incarnate existence, and move to more complex bodies as we get to the point of being able to handle them. That process continues straight through the various kinds of living bodies on Earth. So at any given time, there are a good many human beings who are in their first ever human life, having "graduated" from other living forms; at any given time, there are other humans who have had some human lives already and are starting to get a clue; and there are some who are wrapping up loose ends and getting ready to move on to better things.

There are a lot of bodies being born, though, and the interval between lives has gotten relatively short -- short enough that not all souls manage to process the experiences of one life before they're born into another. I've come to think that that's one of the things behind the number of people with gender dysphoria these days -- going from a body of one gender to a body of another, with too little time to process in between, could definitely result in that feeling of "being in the wrong body."

comments.title: Compassion Vs Pity
comments.content:
I noticed that in order to mask and bury my hatred for my parents, I have contrived different systems of 'understanding' and 'pity' in order to get along with them and wish them only good will in my feelings and thoughts.

I have only been coming to realize the depth of my anger and rage towards their (chosen) ignorance of what they did and did not do in my childhood and adolescence, and how that ignorance shall most likely always be present.

I mistakenly confused myself, through prayer, meditations and exercises that I had already forgiven them and accepted them as they are and were, and am now looking to see what I can do in order to 'actually' accept them that is not dependent on any shift in their position or perspective.

What is 'love' and how can one move towards it?

replies.title: Re: Compassion Vs Pity
replies.content:
I'm going to say something very unpopular here. Compassion, forgiveness and acceptance are seriously overrated, and hatred is a perfectly valid, healthy, and (at times) appropriate emotion. There are times when it's far more honest, and far more healing, to say, "No, I do not accept the people who did this to me. What they did was wrong, and they do not deserve to be forgiven."

This notion that spirituality involves only having soft happy feelings is one of the core factors that have turned religion into the world's most effective cause of atrocities; the return of the repressed is as effective there as it is elsewhere. Accept that what happened to you did in fact happen, sure -- but trying to bully yourself into forgiving something that you know is unforgivable is simply a form of emotional castration. Don't subject yourself to that.

replies.title: Re: Compassion Vs Pity
replies.content:
Here Jesus’s story of the 2 guys who owed money is relevant. For those unfamiliar with the Bible, here’s a paraphrase of the story.

King Thomas had a servant, Dick, who owed him money and didn’t pay. Thomas was going to throw the guy in jail, but Dick begged so hard for mercy that Thomas sighed and forgave the debt. Dick leaves the august presence and meets Harry, who owes HIM money. Harry asks for yet another extension. Dick calls the sheriff and has Harry thrown into debtor’s prison. When this gets back to the king, he summons Dick and says...well, you can fill in what he says.

Father Robert, who taught me my catechism, explained that the meanings of this story are as follows:
1). Don’t be a flaming hypocrite (the moral of many of Jesus’s stories).
2.). Be kind, merciful, and forgiving when you can.
3). Before you refuse to forgive someone, consider that you may want God to forgive YOU someday.

This is actually not incompatible with JMG’s take on the issue. There are some things, e.g. someone killing one of your close relatives, that most people find impossible to forgive. But there are those who have done it. If you’re not one of those saintly folk—well, you tried. Also, “forgiveness” does not necessarily mean you have to like the guy. The king forgave the debt. That didn’t mean he tried to like the servant who stiffed him—it meant he was prepared to shrug and walk away and not carry the guy around in his head for the rest of his life and bore everyone he knew with endless rehashings. (A lot of liberals, for example, would be well advised to forgive the 2016 presidential election.). And on Judgement Day, when God asks, “Why should I forgive you when you didn’t forgive Mr. Jackasd over there?” you will be in a position to reply “ I tried my best,” and God, like any good father, will accept that you did try your best. But he does want you to try. What it all boils down to is walking a mile in the other man’s moccasins, even if the other man is the worst person in the tribe. The tribe may yet end up executing or banishing him, or hating him—but they did consider his side first.

Father Robert was a good, and remorselessly logical, catechist.

replies.title: Re: Compassion Vs Pity
replies.content:
There is a huge difference between forgiveness and acceptance. Forgiveness can help *you* move on-- because holding grudges and continuing to chew over the wrong others have done you will make you crazy. Life is hard. Bad things happen. Some of those things are done to us by others. Often the best we can do is look squarely at what was done to us, what's broken, and do our best to avoid doing that s**t to other people.

Acceptance isn't always warranted. Or safe. Have the people in question *changed*? How much? I mean, you don't have to be angry at the tiger that eats your leg, but you need to stay away from tigers! Some people are so entrenched in their pathologies, that even if they have a "come to Jesus" experience and set about changing themselves for the better, they'll never be decent humans in this life. They're still dangerous, and being around people they've abused before is a temptation akin to a recovering addict meeting up with his old crack dealer. Falling into old patterns is too easy.

Don't accept them as they are. It's not helpful. That's a big, freakish lie of modern times: that accepting people as they are is a good thing. In practice, it's a disaster. Love = wanting people to be better versions of themselves. Not accepting them as they are. I mean, do you prefer friends who accept all your bad habits and help you perpetuate them (and reel you back in when you try to do better!), or friends who recognize that you could do better, and are happy when you manage it?

In my own practice, I find it most helpful to avoid interacting with pathological people as much as possible, but when they repeatedly bubble into my consciousness, I pray for them. I don't know what is good for them, or what they need to become better people-- that's beyond the meager scope of my insight. I say, basically, "God, I don't know what X needs, but you do: please remember X and have mercy on X." After that, it's God's affair, not mine. I don't know if this does anything for the person in question, but it helps *me* tremendously.

replies.content: From an evolutionary standpoint, nothing has a purpose. Evolution is simply the process by which living things adapt to changes in their surroundings; it has no purpose, no goal, and no direction. Civilizations happen when certain factors in a society and its environment make it possible for urban centers and a differentiated workforce to emerge, and they follow the familiar life cycle of rise and fall thereafter; asking what the purpose of a civilization is, in other words, is like asking about the purpose of a tree or a swamp.

replies.title: Re: Training the will
replies.content: Everything in the universe is a limited resource, because limitation is the law of manifestation; what's unlimited is also unmanifest. The thing to watch for is whether a resource is renewable or nonrenewable. Will is a renewable resource, like wind or sunlight; training the will makes you skillful in using your daily supply of will in the most effective and waste-free manner possible.

replies.title: Re: Training the will
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Training the will
replies.content: Do you think maybe you're confusing willpower with something else? For example it can take a lot of effort to break an addiction, or to make a new positive habit stick; over time it gets easier not because you develop more willpower but because you need expend less willpower in maintaining what you've started. So maybe when people talk about building willpower they mean embedding habits of thought which then require less effort to follow, or enable further change to occur at less cost. A lot of smokers spend a lot of effort on self deception or self disdain or open hearted efforts to stop smoking; by embedding the habit either of not smoking or being happy to smoke, they don't evidence increased willpower, only decreased expenditure on daily habit.

comments.title: Scary near death experiences
comments.content: JMG, what do you make of those near death experiences in which people experience something frightening that they interpret as hell or demons? Does occult philosophy have anything to say about this phenomenon? Is it because in some sense it's what these folks expected to see or is there something else going on?

replies.title: Re: Scary near death experiences
replies.content:
The afterlife is not necessarily a pleasant place, especially for unpleasant people. Hell is a reality, though it's not eternal, and demons are as real as angels, ghosts, and gods.

In occult philosophy, once you pass through the second death (the shedding of the etheric body), your soul in its astral body settles out into whatever level of the astral plane the astral body resonates with most strongly. If that's in the lower astral, you're in for a rough ride; if it's in the upper astral, things are considerably more pleasant. Eventually, once you've worked through some of the experiences of the life just past, you also shed the astral body and the soul rises to the mental and then the lowest of the spiritual planes, where it prepares itself for its next plunge into material embodiment.

If you want to stay out of the lower astral after death, reflection, self-knowledge, and an orientation toward spiritual realities are essential. That's what's behind the talk of salvation and damnation you hear in mainstream religions.

replies.title: Re: Scary near death experiences
replies.content:

The degree to which we experience this as transcendent bliss or consuming fire depends entirely on the degree to which we have conformed ourselves to the will of God in this life.

I suppose we'll find out when we get there ;)

comments.title: Really big demons...
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I asked you last year about dealing with people who were employing demons (or perhaps better said, who are in the employ of demons) to harass me. I deeply appreciated your advice, took it to heart and it has helped. Staying out of physical contact with them was a big part of it.

Unfortunately, one of these people live in a dwelling just above me, and their partner (who is the primary consort of the demons) visits several days per week. I have noticed when they are fighting amongst themselves, the partner ups their attacks on me, bringing nastier and nastier beings towards me. There doesn't seem to really be a human presence in this person anymore when it comes to the idea of addressing this person to person. Very sad, and also: not my circus, not my monkeys. I'm staying away as much as possible on the physical plane.

I am banishing every morning, at noon and just before sleep. As a Buddhist, I have enlisted the help of a buddha, two bodhisattvas and a fierce dharmapala, some of whom spontaneously appeared when I first solicited help. I am also calling upon the specific guardian beings who are to help me through this specific time and its trials, to surround me with protection.

Most nights I manage to only notice those demonic creatures buzzing somewhat close, sometimes I will see what look like tracers or trails of white light in my vision (in a wavy pattern) as they try to get in past my defenses. When I see the wavy patterns of light, I invoke protection again, because that means they are getting close. I must also take care not to focus any attention on them, but remain focused on reciting a mantra to one of the beings in my defenses, or other wholesome objects of attention. These demons have been aiming to keep me awake all night through increased heart rate, etc, and sometimes it has felt close to a heart attack or an attempt at other organ interference.

I'm trying to move to another home in a couple of months when my lease ends, but other circumstances may prolong my time here a few more months. I am assuming moving away will end the obsession and pursuit.

But the Full Moons tend to be terrible, as one of them belongs to a kind of coven (who I doubt has any sense around what is going on, either), and I think this person is planning something ridiculous this week. I recently had trouble keeping them out of my field at night, despite multiple methods, and they even invaded my dreams, paralyzing me and I was unable to flee or fight except by awakening.

I'll be glad to be done with this years-long ordeal (I've learned a lot, but kind of done) soon....but are there any special protections you could recommend against an oblivious coven with a member who is conjuring up some very rotten flesh-smelling nasty, icky stuff? And under a Full Moon no less? The recent breaking through has me a little concerned.

Thank you in advance and may all beings benefit.

replies.title: Re: Really big demons...
replies.content:
If I may, how comfortable are you with natural magic?

A few months ago I experienced some much less dramatic magical attacks. Keeping vinegar evaporating by my bed, doing biweekly floorwashes, wearing an amulet as described in the Magic Monday FAQ, daily banishing rituals and washing with protective herbs helped considerably. Hanging bunches of holly, St. John's Wort and other protective herbs around the windows . Wormwood in the mop bucket proved especially helpful, as did a combination of Angelica, Hyssop and Chamomile. Burning thick clouds of Frankincense also helped tremendously. If I were dealing with the same issue now I'd have added in Florida Water a lot faster -- nasty entities really run fast from it! I now put it in my wash water for the floor and my clothing.

Basically, you are involved with spiritual bullies and if you take some natural protective measures you can make it a lot harder for them to attack you. In my experience, multitiered defenses are much better. It reads that you are doing well on the spiritual front with your active religious life. But, that said, it sounds like there may be a lot more etheric and astral defensive measures you can take, as well.

For what it's worth I continue to employ these defense measures because they have the friendly side effect of making me a more balanced and generally nicer person.

Clifford Hartleigh Low has a lot of good insights concerning defense from nasty magic in his comprehensive workshop "Fearless Magical Protection" which can be found here:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GJ0j_ZJVxj4

replies.title: Re: Really big demons...
replies.content:

(I found the salt and nail will discolor within a week, if that tells you anything.) I have the small bowl of vinegar by the bedside, and do a weekly (or twice weekly, depending) hoodoo bath.

May all beings benefit

comments.title: Gareth Knight
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I noticed in a previous Magic Monday you had advised another poster that Gareth Knight's system of magic didn't include a banishing ritual, and thus might carry a higher risk of causing a student to fall into delusion. This is a bit concerning to me as I am currently working through Experience of the Inner Worlds. So far I haven't noticed any negative side effects, but if I did start to experience problems would you recommend incorporating an LBRP into my practice? (I know that Knight includes information on the LBRP in Practical Qabalistic Symbolism, so I'm presuming it would not conflict with the rest of his system) I'm thinking that I'll hold off on doing so unless I do run into problems, given your frequent injunctions to follow magical training courses exactly as presented.

On a more theoretical note, I'm curious what the Sphere of Light exercise lacks that prevents it from performing the function of a banishing ritual. Is it the lack of elemental balancing? The fact that it's entirely mental, without words or gestures of power to anchor it in Malkuth? Something else?

Thanks,

Fred N

replies.title: Re: Gareth Knight
replies.content:
The LBRP would be very well suited to Knight's system; perform it, then sit in a chair at the center of the banishesd space, formulate the Sphere of Light, and proceed from there. You could also use the Sphere of Protection ritual if you prefer.

As to why the Sphere of Light doesn't do the trick, that's simple: you're not actively invoking the protection of one or more divine beings or spiritual powers. The sphere is your creation, and only has as much power as you can give it; by contrast, when you perform the LBRP, you're calling on God using four of his names, and then invoking four mighty archangels to help you.

It might be possible to rework the Sphere of Light by adding in a suitable invocation. All or part of the famous prayer of St. Patrick might be worth trying in this connection.
